item.title: I finally stopped forcing local LLMs and switched back to cloud AI
item.itunes:title: I finally stopped forcing local LLMs and switched back to cloud AI
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
Up until a few months ago, you could've likely gotten away with using free tiers of AI tools exclusively. Most tools didn't have strict limits, and if you hit them, you could just switch to a different tool and keep going. If you hit your Claude limit on the free tier, just switch to your free ChatGPT account. If you ran out there (or weren't getting what you needed with ChatGPT), Gemini was waiting. For a good stretch, that was the workflow for most.

Premium tiers got you more generous limits and access to powerful models (and features earlier than most). The first bit was sorted by bouncing between free tiers of different tools, and the average person didn't really care about getting early access to features. The free stuff worked, and that was enough. Fast-forward to today, and the free tiers of most tools just don't cut it anymore. Claude's free tier has brutal limits, Gemini now has weekly limits too, and the same can be said about ChatGPT. The free-tier rotation technically works, but each tool now also has its strengths, and bouncing between them means giving up the best of each.

Another problem is how expensive running multiple AI subscriptions has become. While paying for the basic version of one Pro plan is fine, those tiers don't really unlock the features that actually justify paying in the first place. Those sit behind the higher tiers, and you typically need to pay upward of $100/month to get them. Ultimately, you might've seen a lot of people running AI models locally on their servers. What most people leave out is that running LLMs locally isn't really for everyone, including me.

First up, you need seriously powerful hardware

Hope you like the sound of fans

The way LLMs work is that they're trained on massive sets of data. The larger the dataset and the more parameters a model has, the smarter it typically is. More parameters also translate to a much bigger model file. Now, with cloud-based LLMs, all this data is stored and processed on the provider's own infrastructure. These are massive data centers packed with the hardware built for this kind of workload. Every time you send a prompt, the hardware does all the work, and you get the response you wanted. There's no load on your device at all, meaning all you really need on your end is a browser and an internet connection.

Want to stay in the loop with the latest in AI? The XDA AI Insider newsletter drops weekly with deep dives, tool recommendations, and hands-on coverage you won't find anywhere else on the site. Subscribe by modifying your newsletter preferences!

Running that same model locally, though, is an entirely different story. Instead of offloading the work to a remote server, you need to host the entire model on your own machine. That essentially means your CPU, GPU, and RAM are doing the work that an entire data center used to handle on your behalf. Every part of the process, including loading the model, processing your prompt, and generating a response, has to happen on your own hardware. And while companies are now clearly directing their efforts toward making smaller and more efficient models that can run on consumer hardware, the reality is that the models worth running still demand a serious machine. In fact, you likely need more powerful hardware than you already have to run some of the smaller models that are actually usable.

The "small" models people recommend for beginners still typically need at least 16GB of RAM to run comfortably, and that's just to get something working. And while the outputs might meet your expectations depending on the task you're performing, the speed at which the model generates responses depends entirely on your hardware. I have an Apple Silicon Mac, and while they're typically known to be good for running LLMs locally, I also only have 8GB of RAM because past Mahnoor didn't know she'd one day want to run LLMs locally on it.

Even the smallest models I've tried run noticeably slower (and typically lead to my Mac running out of application memory midway) than what I'd get from any cloud tool. Anything beyond a quick prompt has me staring at the screen waiting for tokens to appear. So, unless you already have fairly capable hardware or are willing to shell out thousands of dollars, running local LLMs simply isn't a realistic option.

The setup alone is enough to scare most people off

Five hours in and still not a single prompt sent

Given I'm literally someone who writes about tech for a living and is also a Computer Science major, I'm a lot more comfortable with the technical side of things than most people. I'm comfortable with command lines, config files, and just the general weirdness of getting unfamiliar software to run on my machine. I can typically troubleshoot things when they inevitably break, and don't mind digging into documentation and figuring things out as I go when needed.

That said, despite this familiarity, I still feel like a three-year-old who hasn't seen a computer when I hear my colleagues talk about running local LLMs on their own servers. The local-AI rabbit hole goes deeper than most people realize, and all you need to do is glance at one of XDA's articles about self-hosting setups to see what I mean. People are running entire AI stacks on home servers and dedicating Mac Minis to these causes.

And while I could see myself doing all that if the work I do needed all the privacy benefits that come with local LLMs plus the no rate limits and complete control over what I'm running, the reality is that most of what I do day-to-day doesn't need any of that. My own workload doesn't justify the effort, and that realization was enough to send me back to cloud AI without much guilt.

You lose all the features that make cloud AI actually useful

Congrats, you have a chatbot from 2022

Like I mentioned above, the way local LLMs work is that everything they need to function is quite literally installed and contained on your own machine. This includes the knowledge it has, the things it can do, and the limits of what it can respond to. While newer models now have MCP server support and all of the fancy stuff, not every local LLM supports them, and getting any of it to work locally is a project in itself. Plus, with local LLMs, you also lose the entire ecosystem you get with cloud-based AI providers.

My local LLM can call Claude when it's stuck, and it changed everything about my local-first setup

Local LLMs aren't very good on their own

14
By  Anurag Singh

For instance, one of my favorite AI features lately is Projects. Claude and ChatGPT both have it, and NotebookLM is pretty much built around the entire idea of knowledge-based projects. Being able to upload research papers, drafts, transcripts, and reference docs into one space and have the AI work across all of them is exactly the kind of AI application I need in my life. In this case though, it isn't really the model doing the heavy lifting. Rather, it's the entire workflow built around it. And Projects is really just one example. With cloud AI tools that are constantly updated and have huge teams working on them, you're getting a lot more than just access to a model. You get everything cloud providers have built on top of it, and that's where most of what turns a chatbot into something useful for actual work actually lives.

I don't really need the privacy local LLMs promise

The biggest benefits of local LLMs right now seem to be no rate limits and complete privacy. Since the model lives on your machine and never connects to anyone else's servers, nothing you type ever leaves your device. For people working with sensitive data, that alone can be reason enough to put up with all the other tradeoffs. Now, I use AI for studying, as the occasional Grammarly replacement, to vibe-code tools and automations for my workflow, as a research partner, to bounce ideas off when I'm writing, etc.

None of that involves data that is even remotely sensitive, or that I'd be uncomfortable with a company seeing. While rate limits are certainly a problem, I'd rather pay for a higher tier of the cloud tools I'm already using than fight with local hardware just to avoid them. Even at $100/month, I'm still spending less than I would on a machine capable of running the models I'd actually want to use locally. I might change my mind when local LLMs get even better, but for now, cloud AI wins for me.

item.title: Microsoft quietly added a way to scrub Copilot off your PC to the Group Policy
item.itunes:title: Microsoft quietly added a way to scrub Copilot off your PC to the Group Policy
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
Summary

  • Microsoft pulled back Copilot in 2026 after weak user uptake, shifting from an aggressive rollout.
  • New Windows 11 Group Policy "Remove Microsoft Copilot app" deletes Copilot and keeps it off.
  • Windows Home lacks Group Policy, but Registry edits achieve a similar effect.

Microsoft has been approaching 2026 with a far different mindset than the year before. 2025 was packed full of instances where Microsoft rolled out its AI assistant, Copilot, into as many apps and services as possible. However, after the Windows user base failed to meet Microsoft's enthusiasm for Copilot (going so far as to coin the term 'Microslop' around the turn of the new year), the company has been pulling back its efforts.

We've seen Copilot in Notepad and Paint get a revision, and the tech has allowed people to hide the Copilot button in Microsoft 365 in the ribbon if they want. Now, someone has spotted a new entry in the Windows 11 Group Policy that not only gets rid of Copilot, but keeps it off your PC for good.

Microsoft finally admits the Copilot key wasn't a great idea, and it'll let you change it back soon

Better late than never.

1
By  Simon Batt

A new Windows 11 Group Policy lets you get rid of Copilot

It's not a great fix, but it'll do

Windows Latest spotted the new entry in the Group Policy, and believes that it was added sometime in the April 2026 update. While you can manually uninstall Copilot, you may notice it reappearing after an update. Windows Latest claims that this Group Policy will delete Copilot and ensure it doesn't pop back up again.

If you're using a version of Windows 11 that isn't the Home edition, you can open Group Policy and go to User Configuration > Administrative Templates > Windows Components > Windows AI. You'll then see a policy titled "Remove Microsoft Copilot app" that not only removes the desktop version, but also Microsoft 365's Copilot.

Unfortunately, if you're using a Windows Home edition, you won't have the Group Policy tool on your system. However, Windows Latest says you can achieve a similar goal using the Registry editor:

  • Open HKEY_CURRENT_USER\Software\Policies\Microsoft\Windows in the Registry editor.
  • Right-click the Windows key and pick New > Key, then call the new key WindowsAI.
  • Now go to HKEY_CURRENT_USER\Software\Policies\Microsoft\Windows\WindowsAI, right-click on the right side, and pick New > DWORD (32-bit) Value.
  • Double-click it, set it to 1, then either restart Windows or sign out.

While neither of these methods is as useful as Microsoft giving us an in-system toggle, it's a step in the right direction.

Microsoft's big Copilot rollback continues as Office now lets you move its button to the ribbon

Everything in moderation.

By  Simon Batt

item.title: Discord brings better performance and battery use to Windows on Arm with a native app
item.itunes:title: Discord brings better performance and battery use to Windows on Arm with a native app
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
Summary

  • Discord quietly released a native Windows on Arm (ARM64) build; download via Windows > ARM64.
  • This native app avoids emulation, unlocking better performance and battery life on Arm devices.
  • The release is unannounced—consider it a sneak peek until Discord officially confirms it.

For a while, Windows on Arm devices were in a weird spot. They offered better performance and battery life than typical laptops, but they could only achieve that if the apps supported the Snapdragon architecture. Otherwise, you had to rely on emulation, which didn't really tap into all those juicy benefits of using an Arm chip.

Fortunately, support began to trickle in. We saw companies pledge to design versions of their apps that take advantage of the Snapdragon chip, giving Windows on Arm users the boost they rightfully deserved. Now, Discord has joined the ranks of apps that offer an Arm-based version, although the company itself hasn't even announced it yet.

Steam games finally run better on Linux Arm devices, and you can test them right now

The performance gains look great.

1
By  Simon Batt

Discord just got a native Windows on Arm app

You can try it before Discord announces that it's out

As spotted by Neowin, you can now grab a Windows on Arm-native version of the Discord app. This is usually the part where we take a look at the press release to see what the company has to say about it, but the truth is...there is no press release. At least, not yet.

It seems that someone at Discord HQ has pressed the shiny 'release app' button, and you can now grab the Windows on Arm version on the download page. Just click the button for Windows, then pick "ARM64" in the options that appear. However, Discord has yet to declare that the app has been released, so consider it a sneak peek into how it fares before everyone else even learns about it.

Overall, the app shouldn't look any different from the regular version. However, if you're running a Snapdragon device, you should notice it performs better and uses less battery than before. Give it a spin and see if it fares any better on your hardware than the old version.

Maxon Cinema 4D, ZBrush, and Razer Synapse are all coming to Windows on Arm

Things keep getting better

By  João Carrasqueira

item.title: Linus says Linux's trivial fixes are getting out of hand, and AI reviewers are making it worse
item.itunes:title: Linus says Linux's trivial fixes are getting out of hand, and AI reviewers are making it worse
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
Summary

  • Linus is annoyed: AI-driven bug reports are flooding RC channels with noise.
  • Trivial fixes from AI reviewers are creating late-cycle churn he wants to curb.
  • Linus will reject noncritical fixes now; save them for linux-next or the next merge window.

Before a new version of Linux sees public release, it goes through a few rounds of release candidates. These are purely focused on people giving the kernel a spin, spotting bugs, and reporting them so they can be fixed. Linux's founder, Linus Torvald, noticed a strange upsurge in bug reports since version 7.0, and he quickly deduced that it was because people used AI tools to scour the code and report issues automatically.

Last week, Linus took to the release candidate announcements to lament how AI-generated bug reports were flooding the secure channels, when they'd be better off going through the public ones. Now, it seems that Linus is getting peeved with people throwing any and all fixes they find at the maintainers, regardless of how trivial they are.

Linux developers are getting bombarded with AI-generated bug reports, and Linus isn't happy

You won't like it when Linus isn't happy.

4
By  Simon Batt

Linus Torvalds isn't pleased with how Linux 7.1's release candidates are going

Things are a little too noisy for his liking

Linus sent out a message regarding Linux 7.1's fifth release candidate. Linux kernels usually get seven release candidates before they are released for general use, so as long as nothing game-breaking appears in the next few weeks, it should be released soon.

Linus notes that 7.1 is following the same pattern as 7.0, with more changes than usual being made to the kernel. And, much like with 7.0, it's not due to the kernel being in bad health; it's because AI bug spotters are finding and spotting a lot more issues. So much so, in fact, that Linus is getting a little peeved with the sheer number of trivial issues being reported. As he says:

I'm not entirely happy about it - most of this is totally trivial stuff to random drivers, which obviously makes it all less scary, but at the same time I'm really not convinced the churn is worth it at rc5 time. These things are "fixes", sure, but at the same time a lot of them are simply so irrelevant that I think they'd be better off in a linux-next tree and get merged during the merge window.

So I think I'll start being a bit more hardnosed about this kind of unnecessary churn this late in the game. We are supposed to look for *regressions*. Non-critical fixes to long-standing issues are simply not appropriate for this late in the release cycle.

Later on in the message, he says that "several of these series were triggered by AI code review" and that he'll be a lot stricter with these reviews going forward. He justifies his position by saying that, while trivial fixes have a low chance of causing more problems than they repair, it's a non-zero chance, and he'd prefer people take them to the Linux-next tree for inclusion with the next kernel version.

AI tools may be why Linux 7.0's RC cycle was so choppy, but Linus just shipped it anyway

We may be entering a new era of Linux kernel development.

By  Simon Batt

item.title: The Steam Deck won because Valve solved what Microsoft refuses to fix for gaming handhelds
item.itunes:title: The Steam Deck won because Valve solved what Microsoft refuses to fix for gaming handhelds
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
Gaming handhelds have absolutely fantastic hardware. Devices like the Asus ROG Ally X are marvels of modern engineering, packing 120Hz variable refresh rate screens, 24GB of RAM, and massive batteries into an ergonomic shell that you can literally hold in your hands and take on the go. However, there's a major visual disconnect: the jarring experience of turning the handheld on. You pass through a beautiful blue animation only to be dropped face-first into a microscopic Windows 11 desktop. You are suddenly using a thumbstick to drag a tiny mouse cursor over a closed window button that is literally three pixels wide and impossible to click on.

Hardware vendors solved the portable silicon problem years ago. The real bottleneck holding back the handheld revolution is Microsoft's underestimation of the Windows problem. Windows 11 is fundamentally built on enterprise desktop DNA, and trying to mask it with a console skin is like putting a sports car shell over a tractor chassis. The Steam Deck succeeded because Valve built a bespoke Linux operating system, in the form of SteamOS, around a controller interface. Windows handhelds like the ROG Ally X, the Lenovo Legion Go, and the MSI Claw are hamstrung because Windows treats a gaming console like a miniature office laptop. Microsoft's recent Xbox Mode interface updates are welcome, but they don't fix the underlying OS architecture problems.

I already installed SteamOS on the Asus ROG Ally X, and I regret it

This needs a little longer in the oven..

30
By  Joe Rice-Jones

What are the issues at hand?

Windows was not made to be portable

The reason the Steam Deck has been so successful when compared to other gaming handhelds is that it won the UX war. Valve's Steam Deck feels like a Nintendo Switch, while Windows handhelds feel like IT projects. Valve used Linux to create a completely isolated compositor, Gamescope. This means that the desktop doesn't necessarily exist unless you explicitly look for it. Instead, you can just make use of SteamOS and its super easy-to-use and functional interface. This doesn't mean you're restricted from doing whatever you like on your Steam Deck; the option to access the desktop is still available. It just means when you're using it for its fundamental main purpose, it is so much easier to do so, to the point where it makes other handhelds feel like they're ten steps behind.

Another absolutely fantastic feature that the Steam Deck implemented is the Suspend/Resume miracle. The single greatest hand-held feature is the instant sleep and wake. Because Valve controls the software stack, tapping the power button instantly freezes the game state. However, on Windows, tapping the power button triggers standard connected standby, frequently resulting in games crashing, audio looping, or the device waking up inside a carrying case and cooking itself. When you're using a Windows gaming handheld, you just don't have the same benefits as SteamOS has when using Valve's Steam Deck.

Windows handhelds have some major problems, but they can be distilled into three key symptoms. The first is launcher fragmentation. On a console, you push a button and play. However, on a Windows handheld, you have to launch a game from Steam, which opens EA Play, which prompts an update, which steals Windows focus, which hides the on-screen keyboard. It feels like the list just never ends. It's exhausting and frustrating, and even though you finally have the option to play games across multiple launchers rather than being restricted to Steam itself, you might suddenly feel like you don't actually want to because of this fragmentation.

Another issue is the anti-cheat and driver overhead. Windows handhelds are plagued by background OS telemetry, Windows Update interrupting a game session to install a Bluetooth driver, and aggressive kernel-level anti-cheats dragging down CPU thread scheduling on limited battery power. This is something you simply don't face on SteamOS or on consoles, making playing on a Windows gaming handheld a frustrating experience.

4 reasons a Steam Deck shouldn’t replace your dedicated gaming PC

We're evaluating why Valve's Steam Deck isn't a worthwhile replacement for a dedicated gaming PC

2
By  Andre Revilla

Lastly, there is the fact that you're playing navigational whack-a-mole. Standard Windows system dialogues, such as UAC prompts or administrative pop-ups, as well as file explorer errors, do not support controller navigation. The second of these errors occurs: the console illusion vanishes, and you're stuck poking a touchscreen in frustration or using your joysticks to move a tiny cursor around your screen.

Microsoft has tried to catch up

But they're still losing the race

Ever since more and more handhelds have hit the market, it feels like users' complaints about Windows 11 on these devices have only increased. This has led to Microsoft's emergency rescue in the form of the Xbox mode patch. Microsoft recently pushed a massive update expanding a controller-first UI across the OS ecosystem. Essentially, this should have addressed many of the qualms users had with their handheld devices.

First, in late 2025, was the full-screen experience. Microsoft shipped out an aggressive handheld preview that suppresses standard notifications and hides the traditional taskbar whenever a controller-activated environment is booted. Later on in April 2026 was the Xbox Mode realignment, where Microsoft rebranded the interface to Xbox mode, rolling out a unified controller-first dashboard that consolidates Steam, Xbox App, Epic, and GOG libraries into a navigable console-esque tile screen.

The current state is that the OS introduces native background workload deferral, letting the CPU prioritize the active game thread and allocate dedicated system memory profiles specifically for handheld APUs. This has fixed the surface of the problems I outlined above, but this doesn't mean that the issue is completely resolved.

An interface skin just doesn't fix broken foundations. New performance-saving features like Automatic Super Resolution/Auto SR are finally hitting handhelds like the Ryzen AI-powered Lines, but they require deeply integrated NPU hardware. This means that the ecosystem is completely gated and cannot be made use of.

5 reasons I use SteamOS instead of Windows on the ROG Ally

Sorry, but SteamOS is just better.

1
By  Jacob Roach

Alongside this, even with Xbox Mode active, the system layer is still Windows, so if a game crashes to the desktop, Xbox Mode cannot gracefully handle the state change. You are still left looking at a broken background process, forcing the user to touch the screen, swipe, or resort to a third-party overlay like Asus Armory Crate or Lenovo Space just to kill the task. This means, despite all of their attempts, the Windows problem still subsides on gaming handhelds. It's easier to use now, and there are far fewer bouts of friction, but this hasn't solved the problem entirely whatsoever. Unfortunately, the Steam Deck still wins.

Silicon triumphs but software stumbles

The OS has let down gaming handhelds

Motherboard and chipmakers gave us the handheld future we wanted; however, Microsoft is still trying to catch up. A beautiful dashboard launcher cannot fix an operating system that still thinks it's running on a corporate office desk. Realistically, Microsoft needs to stop treating handhelds as a niche hardware form factor and release a lightweight, modular fork of Windows, which is a true Windows Portable Edition. This should delete the legacy desktop codebase entirely. Until they do this, the Steam Deck software dominance remains completely untouched and will do so for years to come.

However, on Windows, tapping the power button triggers standard connected standby, frequently resulting in games crashing, audio looping, or the device waking up inside a carrying case and cooking itself. When you're using a Windows gaming handheld, you just don't have the same benefits as SteamOS has when using Valve's Steam Deck.

Windows handhelds have some major problems, but they can be distilled into three key symptoms. The first is launcher fragmentation. On a console, you push a button and play. However, on a Windows handheld, you have to launch a game from Steam, which opens EA Play, which prompts an update, which steals Windows focus, which hides the on-screen keyboard. It feels like the list just never ends. It's exhausting and frustrating, and even though you finally have the option to play games across multiple launchers rather than being restricted to Steam itself, you might suddenly feel like you don't actually want to because of this fragmentation.

Another issue is the anti-cheat and driver overhead. Windows handhelds are plagued by background OS telemetry, Windows Update interrupting a game session to install a Bluetooth driver, and aggressive kernel-level anti-cheats dragging down CPU thread scheduling on limited battery power. This is something you simply don't face on SteamOS or on consoles, making playing on a Windows gaming handheld a frustrating experience.

4 reasons a Steam Deck shouldn’t replace your dedicated gaming PC

We're evaluating why Valve's Steam Deck isn't a worthwhile replacement for a dedicated gaming PC

2
By  Andre Revilla

Lastly, there is the fact that you're playing navigational whack-a-mole. Standard Windows system dialogues, such as UAC prompts or administrative pop-ups, as well as file explorer errors, do not support controller navigation. The second of these errors occurs: the console illusion vanishes, and you're stuck poking a touchscreen in frustration or using your joysticks to move a tiny cursor around your screen.

Microsoft has tried to catch up

But they're still losing the race

Ever since more and more handhelds have hit the market, it feels like users' complaints about Windows 11 on these devices have only increased. This has led to Microsoft's emergency rescue in the form of the Xbox mode patch. Microsoft recently pushed a massive update expanding a controller-first UI across the OS ecosystem. Essentially, this should have addressed many of the qualms users had with their handheld devices.

First, in late 2025, was the full-screen experience. Microsoft shipped out an aggressive handheld preview that suppresses standard notifications and hides the traditional taskbar whenever a controller-activated environment is booted. Later on in April 2026 was the Xbox Mode realignment, where Microsoft rebranded the interface to Xbox mode, rolling out a unified controller-first dashboard that consolidates Steam, Xbox App, Epic, and GOG libraries into a navigable console-esque tile screen.

The current state is that the OS introduces native background workload deferral, letting the CPU prioritize the active game thread and allocate dedicated system memory profiles specifically for handheld APUs. This has fixed the surface of the problems I outlined above, but this doesn't mean that the issue is completely resolved.

An interface skin just doesn't fix broken foundations. New performance-saving features like Automatic Super Resolution/Auto SR are finally hitting handhelds like the Ryzen AI-powered Lines, but they require deeply integrated NPU hardware. This means that the ecosystem is completely gated and cannot be made use of.

5 reasons I use SteamOS instead of Windows on the ROG Ally

Sorry, but SteamOS is just better.

1
By  Jacob Roach

Alongside this, even with Xbox Mode active, the system layer is still Windows, so if a game crashes to the desktop, Xbox Mode cannot gracefully handle the state change. You are still left looking at a broken background process, forcing the user to touch the screen, swipe, or resort to a third-party overlay like Asus Armory Crate or Lenovo Space just to kill the task. This means, despite all of their attempts, the Windows problem still subsides on gaming handhelds. It's easier to use now, and there are far fewer bouts of friction, but this hasn't solved the problem entirely whatsoever. Unfortunately, the Steam Deck still wins.

Silicon triumphs but software stumbles

The OS has let down gaming handhelds

Motherboard and chipmakers gave us the handheld future we wanted; however, Microsoft is still trying to catch up. A beautiful dashboard launcher cannot fix an operating system that still thinks it's running on a corporate office desk. Realistically, Microsoft needs to stop treating handhelds as a niche hardware form factor and release a lightweight, modular fork of Windows, which is a true Windows Portable Edition. This should delete the legacy desktop codebase entirely. Until they do this, the Steam Deck software dominance remains completely untouched and will do so for years to come.

item.title: I tried this underrated Google Labs tool to vibe-code my UI designs, and now I regret not using it sooner
item.itunes:title: I tried this underrated Google Labs tool to vibe-code my UI designs, and now I regret not using it sooner
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
Vibe-coding and vibe-designing tools have become their own category, and the bigger names get most of the attention: Replit, Lovable, Bolt, Figma Make, and more recently Claude Design. As someone studying design alongside writing about it, I've been pulled into testing most of them partly out of curiosity, but also because it's not something we can ignore in the UX/UI space for much longer. The appeal isn't even really about replacing design tools either, it's about speeding up workflows or giving yourself a starting point when you're staring at a blank canvas.

Stitch had been sitting in my browser tabs since its big update earlier this year, and I finally opened it properly this week. I went in expecting another version of what I'd already tested, and came out wondering why people aren't talking about it more. Google really shipped one of the more interesting design products of the year and it's flying under the radar undeservedly…

Want to stay in the loop with the latest in AI? The XDA AI Insider newsletter drops weekly with deep dives, tool recommendations, and hands-on coverage you won't find anywhere else on the site. Subscribe by modifying your newsletter preferences!

I tested Claude Code, Codex, Lovable, and Replit side by side, and only one felt ready for real work

May the best AI ship.

12
By  Mahnoor Faisal

Stitch wears many hats

A Google Labs experiment that just got a lot more serious

Stitch runs at stitch.withgoogle.com on a Google account and is still in beta under Google Labs, but that label undersells what it's capable of. Stitch originally launched at Google I/O 2025 as a single-screen text-to-UI demo built on the bones of Galileo AI, which Google had acquired earlier that year. The bigger change came in March 2026 though, when Google rebuilt it into what they're calling an AI-native software design canvas: multi-screen generation, an infinite canvas, voice input, interactive prototyping, and design system imports all landed at once.

It's powered by Gemini, you describe what you want, and Stitch generates whole screens, or up to five connected screens in a single prompt. There are two model modes behind the prompt box: a faster Standard mode and an Experimental mode that's slower but produces noticeably better quality. The free tier gives you 350 Standard generations and 200 Experimental generations a month, which is actually a lot more wiggle room than most AI design tools offer without a card on file.

The framing Google is leaning into is "vibe design", where you describe a feeling or intent and let the tool handle the structural decisions. Which puts Stitch closer to a creative brainstorming assistant than either a manual editor or vibe-coding tool, and is part of why it slots so naturally next to Claude Design and Figma Make in this conversation.

The workspace itself reflects this hybrid identity. Opening Stitch puts you on a project dashboard with built-in examples, your past projects, and a prompt box with an App/Web toggle. Once you're inside a project the layout shifts into the left panel for the chat and agent log, middle is the canvas where your screens live, and a right-side toolbar with icons for manual control. So it looks like a chatbot a first, but behaves more like a design tool the longer you're in it.

Then there's the DESIGN.md file, an agent-friendly markdown file that captures your design system in a portable format. You can extract one from any URL or export it out of Stitch and drop it into Claude Code, Cursor, or any other AI coding agent that reads context files. This is not quite where my workflow is right now, but the fact that it exists tells you Stitch isn't really being built as a closed design tool, but rather a design layer that can talk to coding agents. Google considered the entire design-development pipeline.

I use Claude Code and Codex together, and the combination does something neither can do alone

Match made in heaven.

12
By  Mahnoor Faisal

Paste in a URL to inherit a design system

Skipping the prompt gymnastics

This is honestly one of the features that kept me preoccupied. You can paste any publicly accessible URL into the prompt box and Stitch will use that site as the visual reference for whatever it generates. It takes about a minute to crawl the page, and what it's actually doing is parsing the live site for design tokens, so things like hex colors, corner radiuses, padding values, typography, and visual hierarchy. Then it builds a custom design system out of them. So it takes it a step further from screenshotting your inspiration, and reads the rendered page directly. Live, publicly accessible pages work best; anything behind a login or paywall is a no-go.

I used mobbin.com as my reference, which is a design inspiration library and probably the perfect use case for this. Stitch pulled the aesthetic without me having to describe a single color, and every screen it generated afterwards inherited that visual language without me spelling it out. You can drop in any site, like a brand you admire or your own existing site. Stitch is only pulling design tokens, not content - so it's not taking the copy, images, or logos, just the structural and stylistic DNA. Which is basically the same posture as a designer being inspired by a site.

You can edit the design

Manual control woven into the AI

For a tool built around prompting, Stitch gives you more hands-on control than you'd expect, but it's not control in the traditional design tool sense. It's more like having a few knobs and switches the AI lets you operate directly. Most of my fiddling happens in the palette panel on the right, which opens up a full design system editor: light/dark toggle, seed color, primary/secondary/tertiary/neutral swatches, font picker, and corner radius. Tweaking any of these will have the project re-skin to match, no prompting required.

The slash command menu is the other thing that gives you more control. You get a dropdown of things you can add to a screen without writing a full prompt: Image, Logo, Diagram, Animate, App Store Assets, Web Assets, Marketing Kit, Accessibility Audit. The Animate one is genuinely cool. I actually gave it a prompt with my preferred Bezier curve and it gave me an animation that matched in one minute.

Subscribe to the AI Design Newsletter for Tool Insights

Keep up with AI design innovation by subscribing to the newsletter for deep dives into tools like Stitch, hands-on testing, tool recommendations, and practical analysis you can use to assess new AI-driven design products and workflows.
By subscribing, you agree to receive newsletter and marketing emails, and accept our Terms of Use and Privacy Policy. You can unsubscribe anytime.

The Direct Edit pencil tool even lets you click on any text and rewrite it or swap an image. These direct edits don't cost credits, unlike full regenerations, which is huge if you're on the free tier and don't want to burn a generation on a typo. And if you want the AI to edit one specific element rather than the whole screen, you can select that element first and prompt from there. Stitch will regenerate only that screen and tends to respect your intent more reliably than prompting from the main chat.

I tested every AI vibe-coding tool to finish a real project, and they all choke at the same point

Great for prototypes, bad for products

1
By  Nolen Jonker

The prototype builds itself

The flow becomes a launchpad for more screens

Once you've got two or more screens on the canvas, you can wire them up into an interactive prototype - click and drag to connect, hit Play to preview. The part I didn't expect is that Stitch can auto-generate the next logical screen based on tappable elements it sees. Right-click a screen for Instant Prototype, or select a button and ask Stitch to imagine the screen after clicking it. So the prototype isn't just linking what already exists, it's basically a launchpad for more generation.

Stitch deserves more airtime

Stitch slots into the vibe-design conversation quite impressively for something still wearing the Labs label. The URL import alone earns its place in my stack, and the manual controls and prototyping fill in most of what I'd normally bounce around multiple design tools for. It's free, pretty generous, and very underrated in this category of tools. Definitely worth giving a shot (at least, before Google decides to start charging for it).

item.title: I stopped dual-booting Linux after discovering Windows' hidden kernel
item.itunes:title: I stopped dual-booting Linux after discovering Windows' hidden kernel
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
Microsoft tested a lot of new stuff after the Windows 10 release, and the Windows Subsystem for Linux (WSL) and Windows Subsystem for Android are two prime examples. WSA had a rocky start and didn't last long before Microsoft axed the project. However, WSL continued to survive and improve, and with the WSL 2 update, things have gotten better. I am not new to WSL and have found the idea of running a real Linux machine with a Linux kernel on a Windows PC quite fascinating. It is lighter than standard virtualization, and most people don't even know that Windows ships with it.

I leveraged this native implementation to build a Paperless-ngx server that integrates with an AI LLM on an Ubuntu machine. It also serves as a quick-access tool for running Linux apps on Windows when I need them. Let's discuss how the Linux kernel inside your Windows PC can be of great help.

Built-in Linux kernel saves efforts

No more dual-booting or type 2 hypervisors

The traditional way of doing things was to dual-boot Linux alongside Windows. It guaranteed complete access to hardware and avoided the slowdowns associated with virtualization. But dual-booting comes with a risk, because Windows updates have a pattern of breaking the EFI boot order. Once that happens, easy access to your Linux system no longer works and requires troubleshooting. It’s not a huge problem, but no one likes such obstructions.

Virtualization is another way to run Linux on Windows. It shares underlying resources via virtualization technology and can run most Linux distributions within a hypervisor. However, you need a robust system that can handle running two systems at once and still have resources for apps and multitasking. In addition, you have two OSes competing for the same resources, which becomes a problem in the long run as your virtual system’s demand increases.

WSL solves both these problems in a unique style. It includes a Linux kernel and a lightweight virtual machine running Hyper-V. This lightweight virtual machine implementation can access system resources with minimal virtualization stress. Microsoft can optimize it to the point where there’s minimal virtualization overhead, and the system can run fine with the bare minimum of memory and CPU. It’s still virtualization, but WSL runs a real Linux kernel, so apps talk to the kernel directly rather than through a Windows translation layer before accessing hardware via Hyper-V.

I tested dual-boot, WSL2, and VMs to run Linux apps on Windows, and only one was worth keeping

Dual-boot, VMs, and WSL2 walk into a Windows PC...

9
By  Ayush Pande

WSL makes an excellent Docker playground

Minimal Ubuntu install paired with Docker

A traditional virtual machine with a desktop environment needs more storage space and computing power. Since WSL is a terminal-focused tool, it skips the desktop environment and installs only CLI-based distros like Ubuntu. There are quite a few options to choose from, but I prefer Ubuntu due to its familiarity. On one of my Windows machines, I run an Ubuntu system with a Docker setup that hosts a single stack.

It runs a Paperless-ngx server, which serves as a document management platform for every transaction I make or every document I acquire. The whole stack runs inside Docker, and I can call and control it via the WSL terminal. Since it has a web dashboard, I rarely interact with it via WSL, unless I need to make some configuration changes.

The next piece of the puzzle is a local LLM that runs inside the Ubuntu system and combines with the Paperless-ngx instance. I use Ollama for this role, and it powers multiple features such as automatic processing, summaries, and custom chats with each document or the entire database.

WSL 2 makes GPU para-virtualization a breeze, and Ollama can access my NVIDIA RTX 3060 GPU without any custom configuration. If I picked a type 2 hypervisor, I would have to figure out the GPU passthrough process and install the correct drivers, but WSL 2 does all that when I set up the machine. I don’t need to do anything else except create a machine and then install Ollama and my Docker container stack on it. WSL 2 excels at CUDA tasks, and Ollama benefits from this optimization.

I can find any document that I need using a local AI assistant. It can understand natural language and find files and information in minutes, compared to the minutes it would take me to browse categories and tags in my Paperless-ngx server. In addition, my data stays on my system, and I don’t need to pay for monthly API subscriptions.

Running Linux tools on Windows

No switching needed

If I use dual-booting or virtual machines, I have to switch back and forth between Windows and Linux. But WSL makes it possible to use Linux tools on Windows via the terminal. I can use any Linux CLI tool directly from the terminal without breaking my current workflow. No need to shut down or restart the system, or temporarily leave it to access the same tool inside a VMware or other hypervisor machine.

Deals

Deals on PCs, GPUs, and work-setup gear - big savings

Explore discounts across computers, high-performance GPUs, SSDs, RAM, docks, and monitors to build or upgrade a development workstation. Compare offers on accessories and peripherals to stretch your budget and boost performance for containers, local AI, and self-hosting.
Deals Explore Computers & Work Setup Deals

Similarly, WSLg makes it possible to run GUI apps on Windows via Weston. It can run and display apps as they would load on a Linux machine, and that’s one of the biggest benefits for users who prefer some Linux-only apps. It’s possible to get a cross-platform experience where you can run a niche tool like the GNOME Disks app present in Ubuntu on Windows or any other similar GUI tool.

WSL changes everything

I relied on dual-booting before switching to type 2 hypervisors and then finally settled on WSL. It’s been a long journey, and WSL fits my current need to try out tools on my Windows systems and control them from a single terminal. Dual-booting is relevant when you want full control over hardware, but if you intend to run Linux and self-hosted tools on Windows, WSL makes it easy. You don’t need to learn the nuances of virtualization or fiddle with settings to get the GPU and other components working correctly.
item.description:
Microsoft tested a lot of new stuff after the Windows 10 release, and the Windows Subsystem for Linux (WSL) and Windows Subsystem for Android are two prime examples. WSA had a rocky start and didn't last long before Microsoft axed the project. However, WSL continued to survive and improve, and with the WSL 2 update, things have gotten better. I am not new to WSL and have found the idea of running a real Linux machine with a Linux kernel on a Windows PC quite fascinating. It is lighter than standard virtualization, and most people don't even know that Windows ships with it.

I leveraged this native implementation to build a Paperless-ngx server that integrates with an AI LLM on an Ubuntu machine. It also serves as a quick-access tool for running Linux apps on Windows when I need them. Let's discuss how the Linux kernel inside your Windows PC can be of great help.

Built-in Linux kernel saves efforts

No more dual-booting or type 2 hypervisors

The traditional way of doing things was to dual-boot Linux alongside Windows. It guaranteed complete access to hardware and avoided the slowdowns associated with virtualization. But dual-booting comes with a risk, because Windows updates have a pattern of breaking the EFI boot order. Once that happens, easy access to your Linux system no longer works and requires troubleshooting. It’s not a huge problem, but no one likes such obstructions.

Virtualization is another way to run Linux on Windows. It shares underlying resources via virtualization technology and can run most Linux distributions within a hypervisor. However, you need a robust system that can handle running two systems at once and still have resources for apps and multitasking. In addition, you have two OSes competing for the same resources, which becomes a problem in the long run as your virtual system’s demand increases.

WSL solves both these problems in a unique style. It includes a Linux kernel and a lightweight virtual machine running Hyper-V. This lightweight virtual machine implementation can access system resources with minimal virtualization stress. Microsoft can optimize it to the point where there’s minimal virtualization overhead, and the system can run fine with the bare minimum of memory and CPU. It’s still virtualization, but WSL runs a real Linux kernel, so apps talk to the kernel directly rather than through a Windows translation layer before accessing hardware via Hyper-V.

I tested dual-boot, WSL2, and VMs to run Linux apps on Windows, and only one was worth keeping

Dual-boot, VMs, and WSL2 walk into a Windows PC...

9
By  Ayush Pande

WSL makes an excellent Docker playground

Minimal Ubuntu install paired with Docker

A traditional virtual machine with a desktop environment needs more storage space and computing power. Since WSL is a terminal-focused tool, it skips the desktop environment and installs only CLI-based distros like Ubuntu. There are quite a few options to choose from, but I prefer Ubuntu due to its familiarity. On one of my Windows machines, I run an Ubuntu system with a Docker setup that hosts a single stack.

It runs a Paperless-ngx server, which serves as a document management platform for every transaction I make or every document I acquire. The whole stack runs inside Docker, and I can call and control it via the WSL terminal. Since it has a web dashboard, I rarely interact with it via WSL, unless I need to make some configuration changes.

The next piece of the puzzle is a local LLM that runs inside the Ubuntu system and combines with the Paperless-ngx instance. I use Ollama for this role, and it powers multiple features such as automatic processing, summaries, and custom chats with each document or the entire database.

WSL 2 makes GPU para-virtualization a breeze, and Ollama can access my NVIDIA RTX 3060 GPU without any custom configuration. If I picked a type 2 hypervisor, I would have to figure out the GPU passthrough process and install the correct drivers, but WSL 2 does all that when I set up the machine. I don’t need to do anything else except create a machine and then install Ollama and my Docker container stack on it. WSL 2 excels at CUDA tasks, and Ollama benefits from this optimization.

I can find any document that I need using a local AI assistant. It can understand natural language and find files and information in minutes, compared to the minutes it would take me to browse categories and tags in my Paperless-ngx server. In addition, my data stays on my system, and I don’t need to pay for monthly API subscriptions.

Running Linux tools on Windows

No switching needed

If I use dual-booting or virtual machines, I have to switch back and forth between Windows and Linux. But WSL makes it possible to use Linux tools on Windows via the terminal. I can use any Linux CLI tool directly from the terminal without breaking my current workflow. No need to shut down or restart the system, or temporarily leave it to access the same tool inside a VMware or other hypervisor machine.

Deals

Deals on PCs, GPUs, and work-setup gear - big savings

Explore discounts across computers, high-performance GPUs, SSDs, RAM, docks, and monitors to build or upgrade a development workstation. Compare offers on accessories and peripherals to stretch your budget and boost performance for containers, local AI, and self-hosting.
Deals Explore Computers & Work Setup Deals

Similarly, WSLg makes it possible to run GUI apps on Windows via Weston. It can run and display apps as they would load on a Linux machine, and that’s one of the biggest benefits for users who prefer some Linux-only apps. It’s possible to get a cross-platform experience where you can run a niche tool like the GNOME Disks app present in Ubuntu on Windows or any other similar GUI tool.

WSL changes everything

I relied on dual-booting before switching to type 2 hypervisors and then finally settled on WSL. It’s been a long journey, and WSL fits my current need to try out tools on my Windows systems and control them from a single terminal. Dual-booting is relevant when you want full control over hardware, but if you intend to run Linux and self-hosted tools on Windows, WSL makes it easy. You don’t need to learn the nuances of virtualization or fiddle with settings to get the GPU and other components working correctly.

Explore discounts across computers, high-performance GPUs, SSDs, RAM, docks, and monitors to build or upgrade a development workstation. Compare offers on accessories and peripherals to stretch your budget and boost performance for containers, local AI, and self-hosting.

Similarly, WSLg makes it possible to run GUI apps on Windows via Weston. It can run and display apps as they would load on a Linux machine, and that’s one of the biggest benefits for users who prefer some Linux-only apps. It’s possible to get a cross-platform experience where you can run a niche tool like the GNOME Disks app present in Ubuntu on Windows or any other similar GUI tool.

WSL changes everything

I relied on dual-booting before switching to type 2 hypervisors and then finally settled on WSL. It’s been a long journey, and WSL fits my current need to try out tools on my Windows systems and control them from a single terminal. You don’t need to learn the nuances of virtualization or fiddle with settings to get the GPU and other components working correctly.

item.title: Your next PC upgrade should probably be storage organization, not faster storage
item.itunes:title: Your next PC upgrade should probably be storage organization, not faster storage
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
If you are looking to upgrade your PC, storage shouldn't be your next priority, especially if you are already using an NVMe SSD. At a time when SSD prices are near an all-time high, you should focus on better storage organization. Modern SSDs are already fast enough, and a hardware upgrade won't bring any meaningful or noticeable change to performance for the average user, but better organization can.

The most important thing is ensuring enough free storage space on the SSD, ideally 10-20%. Anything under that and the system gradually starts to slow down. Towards the end, when there's not much breathing room left, you will start seeing errors and warnings. But it should never come down to that, and with systematic storage organization, you can keep your system running as fast as new for years. I have been using a 512GB NVMe SSD for five years now, and I haven't considered upgrading. Because smart management of available resources matters more than chasing benchmarks.

Modern SSDs are already fast enough

At least for everyday users

In the past few years, we have reached a stage where storage speeds are no longer the performance bottleneck, at least for the average user. Even when it comes to gaming, upgrading to an NVMe Gen5 SSD won't result in any noticeable boost. The same applies to other apps you run on the PC. Storage speeds are just one of the bottlenecks, but that's what often gets the most attention. Your RAM and CPU are just as important, and unless those are upgraded too, you are unlikely to benefit from switching to a faster SSD.

The read and write speeds you see in disk benchmarking software like CrystalDiskMark don't always help. Even with high numbers there, the PC might still struggle with performance if the storage remains poorly organized. A jump to 12,000 MB/s, though it sounds huge on paper, is unlikely to translate into anything of value for everyday users if the drive ultimately ends up cluttered and disorganized.

Your old SSD might be slowing down — here’s the hidden settings I changed

It's time for change.

1
By  Rich Edmonds

Cluttered, unorganized drives are the real problem

This impacts performance, responsiveness, and usability

For the longest time, I never really cared about what was on my storage drive. As long as the PC wasn't throwing any errors or warnings, I assumed everything was fine. From a purely technical standpoint, it was. Apps launched and worked, all the files were accessible, and the few games I had never really showed signs of slowdown. Then, one day, I deleted all irrelevant files, found and removed duplicates, cleared the app cache, and moved rarely used files to an external HDD, and the system performance improved immediately. Until then, my "Downloads" folder was a mess, my desktop had over 30 icons, and every app saved files to the directory it liked.

For gamers, the problem becomes even bigger, as modern titles easily eat up over 100GB of storage. Get a few of these, and your system starts to struggle. For the uninitiated, moving older games to an HDD is a smart approach. I am a big fan of old games, especially the early versions of Age of Empires, and I have realized that they work just as well on an HDD as on an SSD. It likely stems from the fact that these games were developed when HDDs were still the common storage medium.

If this applies to you, the first thing you should do is move such games or titles you occasionally play to an external storage drive. This would naturally free up maximum storage. And if the game doesn't run as well, you can always move it back to the primary SSD. At least this way, you will figure out which games actually benefit from being stored on an SSD and the ones that don't.

3 reasons you should have a dedicated SSD just for games

Keeping your OS and games on separate SSDs might actually make sense

6
By  Tanveer Singh

Better storage organization can be a game-changer

With SSD prices climbing, optimize what you already have

Storage organization may sound like a boring, tedious task, and frankly, it is. Though you can automate parts of it, you will still need to verify occasionally that everything is working properly. But it's one of the best things you could do for your PC. It improves performance without costing you a dime. At a time when PC components are getting expensive due to increased demand, such optimizations are more useful than ever.

The next thing I did after moving games to the external SSD was to organize my "Downloads" folder. It had accumulated so many files, including 2-3 Windows ISO for different iterations and countless installers, over the years, that finding a simple PDF took longer than it ideally should.

Once I was done cleaning up, I even changed the default location of the "Downloads" folder to improve storage organization and free up space on the system drive. It was then time to free up space on the PC. As surprising as it may sound, this is not a difficult task. With built-in tools like "Disk Cleanup" and "Storage Sense" and third-party utilities like "WizTree," you can quickly free up more than 50GB on a heavily cluttered drive.

Deals

Score Storage Deals: Save on SSDs, HDDs & NAS

Unlock major savings on storage and networking deals - from external HDDs and NVMe SSDs to NAS units, enclosures, and backup accessories. Browse discounts and bundles to expand capacity, streamline backups, and protect data without breaking the bank.
Deals Explore Storage & Networking Deals

Once I had the core set up, I moved on to storage organization and optimization. That, frankly, included a lot of things, from pinning commonly used files to the Quick Access menu and apps to the Taskbar, to creating a folder structure that made sense to me.

Honestly, this isn't a one-time thing; you will need to pin/unpin apps and folders depending on what you are working on. I still run a monthly check to make sure everything is in order, that files aren't being stored in directories I don't want them in, and that everything I need is easily accessible. There's no right or wrong here. It all comes down to how you use the PC. But the aim is to streamline access and usability.

5 SSD myths that are simply untrue

Don't fall for these common SSD misunderstandings.

12
By  Jacob Roach

Faster storage still works, but not as much as you think

Having said all that, it's also important to realize that faster storage is not pointless. If you have the resources and can benefit from upgrading, it makes sense. But for the average user, someone who runs a handful of apps at once or isn't into competitive gaming, saving a second or two (at most) may not be worth the cost. Perhaps when (and if) prices stabilize down the line, you could upgrade to faster storage, but for now, storage organization is perhaps the smarter choice.

Having said all that, it's also important to realize that faster storage is not pointless. If you have the resources and can benefit from upgrading, it makes sense. But for the average user, someone who runs a handful of apps at once or isn't into competitive gaming, saving a second or two (at most) may not be worth the cost. Perhaps when (and if) prices stabilize down the line, you could upgrade to faster storage, but for now, storage organization is perhaps the smarter choice.

item.title: 6 underrated Apple CarPlay apps and widgets I use for a distraction-free drive
item.itunes:title: 6 underrated Apple CarPlay apps and widgets I use for a distraction-free drive
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
Our car dashboard has become the final frontier for our digital attention. Apple CarPlay is a powerful tool, but without a deliberate setup, it can quickly become just another screen competing for your focus while you are behind the wheel. That’s why I have streamlined my interface to prioritize only what is essential: seamless navigation, effortless audio control, and glanceable information.

Here are the apps and widgets I use to transform my dashboard into a distraction-free environment that keeps my eyes where they belong – on the road.

ChatGPT

Voice-first AI research partner

ChatGPT has become a central part of my workflow, so when it officially arrived on CarPlay, it immediately earned a spot on my home screen. It’s essentially a hands-free research partner; I use it to brainstorm article outlines or go over causal queries during my commute without ever touching my phone.

Since it’s purely voice-based and doesn’t display text on the screen, it fits perfectly into my goal of a distraction-free drive. I can talk through a tricky paragraph or ask for a quick summary of a concept, and everything is saved in my chat history for me to review once I’m back at my desk.

3 important ways CarPlay is better than Android Auto

Google's in-car infotainment system offers customization and apps, but CarPlay is cohesive and reliable.

6
By  Patrick O'Rourke

Google Meet

Streamlined audio-only conferencing

While I generally treat the car as a no-meeting zone, there are days when a sync is unavoidable. Google Meet on CarPlay has been a surprisingly helpful addition to my dashboard, especially because it treats meetings like phone calls. When I join a scheduled session, the interface is stripped back – there is no video feed to tempt my eyes away from the windshield. It just offers larger, clearer buttons for muting, unmuting, and hanging up.

I have found it useful for those weekly editorial syncs where I mainly need to listen in on the upcoming schedule; I can stay looped into the conversation via my car’s audio system while keeping my hands on the wheel and my focus on the traffic.

Fuelio

Effortless fuel and mileage tracking

Fuelio has become my go-to for managing the business side of owning a car right from the driver’s seat. Recently, they have added CarPlay support to focus on exactly what I need when I’m at the pump or on a long journey.

I use it primarily to track my mileage and fuel costs without having to fumble with my phone or a paper logbook at the petrol station.

It’s a small addition to my dashboard. I also use the CarPlay map view within Fuelio to find the nearest gas stations and check fuel prices. It saves me from having to pull over and search on a phone app when I’m running low.

Microsoft To Do

Glanceable daily task manager

Microsoft To Do is a must-have widget for my CarPlay setup. I have pinned it directly to my CarPlay dashboard so I can see my most pressing tasks at a glance.

Instead of wondering if I remembered to send that final draft or check a specific spec for a review, I can just look at the widget during a red light. It provides that instant peace of mind. While Microsoft To Do is my preferred choice, you can also use the default Reminders app widget for the same purpose. It even works with Siri voice assistant.

Asana

Professional project workload snapshot

Asana has been our team’s command center for years, so it was a major win when I figured out how to bring that bird’s-eye view into the car. I have pinned the Asana widget right next to my navigation on the CarPlay dashboard, and it has changed how I mentally gear up for the day.

Instead of checking my phone before I pull out of the driveway, I can just glance at the screen to see exactly how many tasks are on my plate for the week. It’s a subtle but powerful visual cue. If it’s a heavy week, I use my commute for a bit more quiet time or focused brainstorming with ChatGPT.

Ambient Music

One-tap relaxing soundscapes

After a heavy day of writing and digging into complex datasets, the last thing I want to do is navigate through the infinite choice paralysis of YouTube Music. That’s why I have made the Ambient Music widget a permanent fixture on my CarPlay dashboard. It’s the ultimate reset button for my commute.

Subscribe to the newsletter for smarter CarPlay setups

Get hands-on CarPlay recommendations—subscribe to the newsletter for curated app picks, step-by-step widget setups, and distraction-free dashboard strategies you can apply on your commute. Ideal for readers eager to improve in-car productivity.
By subscribing, you agree to receive newsletter and marketing emails, and accept our Terms of Use and Privacy Policy. You can unsubscribe anytime.

I don’t have to search for a specific track or browse through albums; with a single swipe to my widget stack and a quick tap, the system immediately fires up a curated soundscape designed to help me as I drive through the streets of Surat.

CarPlay, simplified

A distraction-free dashboard is all about having the right features in exactly the right places. Whether you are streamlining your commute or prepping for a long road trip, these small adjustments to your CarPlay can make a massive difference in your safety and peace of mind.

Start with the apps and widgets mentioned and see how they change your next commute. And if you are confused between CarPlay and Android Auto, here are the reasons to use Apple’s version over Google’s.

The Android Auto feature nobody talks about that I use on every drive

Go beyond maps and music.

4
By  Parth Shah

item.title: Most people never check this one router setting, and it's why their internet feels slow
item.itunes:title: Most people never check this one router setting, and it's why their internet feels slow
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
Over the past six months, I have been convinced that my router was quietly dying. The pages loaded slowly, video streams buffered every minute, and video calls often dropped. Whenever that happened, I’d run a speed test, take a screenshot of the results, and send it to my ISP, who simply suggested, "Did you try turning the router off and on again?"

The ISP closed several tickets for my slow internet and stated that nothing was officially wrong. I tried switching hardware and even a couple of software tools that promised a speed boost. Nothing worked. So I went down the rabbit hole to troubleshoot my network more deeply and learned that the quality of the DNS server affects internet connection performance. Tweaking that changed everything.

5 router settings most people never touch that fixed my network problems

Lesser-known fixes for well-known problems

10
By  Chandraveer Mathur

Nobody questions or checks the ISP’s default options

You can tweak a few things

Most of us continue using the internet connection once the ISP activates it. Why bother tinkering around with the router’s settings when everything works, right? As the ISP suggested, I’d run speed tests just to check the throughput numbers. There’s no other detail in the results hinting at a potential network problem.

I kept walking through the usual troubleshooting checklist, like cable swaps, router reboots, and ISP support calls. That’s when I discovered that I was using my ISP’s DNS servers. Before a website loads any content, the domain name (URL) must be resolved to an IP address.

I sought a decent DNS benchmark to confirm that my ISP’s DNS servers were the culprits. In the WAN status menu of my router's interface, I saw that it was using an ISP-provided DNS. Before switching, I wanted to measure its efficiency.

I stumbled upon GRC’s DNS Benchmark v2 tool. It offers a one-time $9.95 purchase for professionals and power users who want a deeper, more accurate resolver analysis. I found a web-based DNS Speed Test benchmark that runs DNS-over-HTTPS requests in real time to test DNS servers. The ISP’s servers were well below those of other DNS resolver providers.

Switching to public DNS servers for an immediate fix

Reasonable place for most people

Switching to a public DNS provider is the obvious move. Based on the DNS Speed Test results, I weighed my options:

  • Cloudflare - 1.1.1.1 / 1.0.0.1
  • Google - 8.8.8.8 / 8.8.4.4
  • OpenDNS - 208.67.222.222 /208.67.220.220
  • NextDNS - 45.90.28.232 / 45.90.30.232
  • Quad9 - 9.9.9.9 / 149.112.112.112
  • AdGuard - 94.140.14.140 /94.140.14.141

I didn’t default to the popular option without testing since the DNS performance varies significantly by ISP and region. So I ran the DNS Speed Test a few times from my Windows 11 desktop computer and my laptop to average out the results and identify the best performer. Cloudflare came out on top by a clear margin.

So I swapped out the ISP’s DNS server settings in the router interface with Cloudflare's DNS server addresses. Following that, DNS resolution was faster, as evidenced by faster page loads that opened the site immediately. Also, the media streaming became consistent enough that I stopped running speed tests altogether.

For most home users, swapping out the ISP’s DNS servers for reputable public DNS providers in the router is where the journey stops. That makes the internet connection feel faster on all devices.

​

Running DNS locally with Unbound

Maintenance challenges

Though public DNS providers deliver a faster internet experience, my DNS queries still pass through someone else’s infrastructure. While these public DNS providers have solid privacy policies, I wanted a resolver I could closely control and run on my own hardware.

I run Unbound as a recursive DNS resolver with Pi-hole for local recursion, ad blocking, and privacy, without sending DNS queries to any public DNS providers. Since Unbound is lightweight and talks directly to the root servers, there’s no middleman, no query logs sent to a third party, and I get DNSSEC validation.

This setup runs on my used mini PC, which is running a Debian 13 VM. I ran a trial on a Raspberry Pi 4, and it worked well. I recommend using an SSD for storage instead of a memory card. Once set up, I simply use Pi-hole’s IP address in my router's DNS settings. After that, Pi-hole and Unbound work together to prevent DNS queries from being forwarded to any third-party DNS resolver. The only trade-off is that both require some monitoring and maintenance.

Setting realistic expectations for your own DNS stack

With control comes responsibility

Running a DNS management stack with Pi-hole and Unbound is just the starting point. It’s not one of those set-and-forget setups — you’ll need to tinker around to whitelist and blacklist domains often. Undoubtedly, the configuration makes you feel like a DNS administrator who keeps the internet running without hiccups, but it also involves some maintenance risk.

Deals

Save on routers, NAS, and networking gear — deals now

Explore storage & networking deals to cut costs on routers, switches, mini PCs, NAS enclosures, SSDs and Raspberry Pi accessories. Shop discounts and limited-time offers to boost home network speed, reliability, and local storage without overspending.
Deals Explore Storage & Networking Deals

If something breaks at midnight, the internet connectivity goes down. Then, you need to troubleshoot Pi-hole and Unbound individually. Both follow a different configuration format and failure modes.

Of the two, Pi-hole is the more approachable tool, with a friendly interface that clearly displays blocklists, query logs, and error logs. But then Pi-hole needs Unbound as an upstream server to handle iterative resolution. Without it, Pi-hole still needs a public DNS provider as a fallback.

You can deploy both in an afternoon and point your router’s DNS settings to the Pi-hole’s IP address. The only drawback is that if one of them goes down, your devices' internet connectivity is affected.

4 reasons you should run your own DNS server with Unbound

Upgrading your network with a self-hosted DNS server is one of the best improvements you can make

4
By  Elliot Alexander

Faster internet is one change away

Switching DNS servers from the ISP’s defaults to a public DNS provider is a five-minute change with a noticeable payoff. If that fixes your internet issues, you’re done. But if you want more control over your privacy, Pi-hole and Unbound on Raspberry Pi can get you there.

Either way, you shouldn’t leave the ISP’s default DNS settings in our router untouched. That’s one setting no one tells you to check, especially when you experience sluggish internet.

  • Pi-hole
    OS
    Linux
    Price model
    Free
    See at Github
  • Unbound
    OS
    Windows, Linux, macOS
    Price model
    Free

    Unbound is an open-source caching, recursive DNS resolver.

    See at NLnet labs

  • Google - 8.8.8.8 / 8.8.4.4
  • OpenDNS - 208.67.222.222 /208.67.220.220
  • NextDNS - 45.90.28.232 / 45.90.30.232
  • Quad9 - 9.9.9.9 / 149.112.112.112
  • AdGuard - 94.140.14.140 /94.140.14.141

I didn’t default to the popular option without testing since the DNS performance varies significantly by ISP and region. So I ran the DNS Speed Test a few times from my Windows 11 desktop computer and my laptop to average out the results and identify the best performer. Cloudflare came out on top by a clear margin.

So I swapped out the ISP’s DNS server settings in the router interface with Cloudflare's DNS server addresses. Following that, DNS resolution was faster, as evidenced by faster page loads that opened the site immediately. Also, the media streaming became consistent enough that I stopped running speed tests altogether.

For most home users, swapping out the ISP’s DNS servers for reputable public DNS providers in the router is where the journey stops. That makes the internet connection feel faster on all devices.

​

Running DNS locally with Unbound

Maintenance challenges

Though public DNS providers deliver a faster internet experience, my DNS queries still pass through someone else’s infrastructure. While these public DNS providers have solid privacy policies, I wanted a resolver I could closely control and run on my own hardware.

I run Unbound as a recursive DNS resolver with Pi-hole for local recursion, ad blocking, and privacy, without sending DNS queries to any public DNS providers. Since Unbound is lightweight and talks directly to the root servers, there’s no middleman, no query logs sent to a third party, and I get DNSSEC validation.

This setup runs on my used mini PC, which is running a Debian 13 VM. I ran a trial on a Raspberry Pi 4, and it worked well. I recommend using an SSD for storage instead of a memory card. Once set up, I simply use Pi-hole’s IP address in my router's DNS settings. After that, Pi-hole and Unbound work together to prevent DNS queries from being forwarded to any third-party DNS resolver. The only trade-off is that both require some monitoring and maintenance.

Setting realistic expectations for your own DNS stack

With control comes responsibility

Running a DNS management stack with Pi-hole and Unbound is just the starting point. It’s not one of those set-and-forget setups — you’ll need to tinker around to whitelist and blacklist domains often. Undoubtedly, the configuration makes you feel like a DNS administrator who keeps the internet running without hiccups, but it also involves some maintenance risk.

If something breaks at midnight, the internet connectivity goes down. Then, you need to troubleshoot Pi-hole and Unbound individually. Both follow a different configuration format and failure modes.

Of the two, Pi-hole is the more approachable tool, with a friendly interface that clearly displays blocklists, query logs, and error logs. But then Pi-hole needs Unbound as an upstream server to handle iterative resolution. Without it, Pi-hole still needs a public DNS provider as a fallback.

You can deploy both in an afternoon and point your router’s DNS settings to the Pi-hole’s IP address. The only drawback is that if one of them goes down, your devices' internet connectivity is affected.

4 reasons you should run your own DNS server with Unbound

Upgrading your network with a self-hosted DNS server is one of the best improvements you can make

4
By  Elliot Alexander

Faster internet is one change away

Switching DNS servers from the ISP’s defaults to a public DNS provider is a five-minute change with a noticeable payoff. If that fixes your internet issues, you’re done. But if you want more control over your privacy, Pi-hole and Unbound on Raspberry Pi can get you there.

Either way, you shouldn’t leave the ISP’s default DNS settings in our router untouched. That’s one setting no one tells you to check, especially when you experience sluggish internet.
